name: Jake LaMotta Fix
description: The "Jake LaMotta Fix" refers to a conspiracy theory alleging that boxer Jake LaMotta deliberately threw his April 29, 1947, fight against Billy Fox in exchange for a future shot at the middleweight title. Proponents claim LaMotta, known for his toughness and fighting prowess, uncharacteristically performed poorly, leading to a suspicious fourth-round TKO loss. They suggest organized crime, specifically the Mafia, orchestrated the fix, manipulating boxing outcomes for financial gain and control. Evidence cited includes LaMotta's own later admission in his autobiography, "Raging Bull," where he confessed to throwing the fight to appease the mobsters controlling boxing at the time. Furthermore, some point to the unusual circumstances surrounding the fight, such as the sudden change in LaMotta's fighting style and the perceived lack of effort, as indicators of a predetermined outcome. Mainstream boxing historians and analysts generally acknowledge LaMotta's admission but often frame it within the context of the corrupt boxing environment of the mid-20th century. They argue that while LaMotta's actions were unethical and illegal, they were also a product of the pervasive influence of organized crime in the sport, where fighters often faced pressure to cooperate with fixers. Critics also highlight the difficulty in definitively proving the extent of Mafia involvement in the fight beyond LaMotta's own testimony, emphasizing the lack of concrete external evidence. Some historians suggest that LaMotta's admission, while credible, might also have been motivated by a desire to enhance his notoriety and marketability.
subtitle: Boxing fix conspiracy theory.

claims.claim: Jake LaMotta intentionally lost to Billy Fox.
claims.evidence: LaMotta's own confession in his autobiography 'Raging Bull'.
claims.criticism: Confession is self-reported, difficult to independently verify all details.

claims.claim: The Mafia orchestrated the fix for financial gain and control over boxing.
claims.evidence: Widespread Mafia influence in boxing during the mid-20th century.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence directly linking the Mafia to this specific fight beyond LaMotta's testimony.

claims.claim: LaMotta's unusual performance during the fight indicates a predetermined outcome.
claims.evidence: Sudden change in fighting style, perceived lack of effort, and questionable refereeing.
claims.criticism: Subjective interpretations of fight footage; LaMotta may have simply had an off night or been outmatched.

keyFigures.name: Jake LaMotta
keyFigures.role: Boxer who confessed to throwing the fight.

keyFigures.name: Billy Fox
keyFigures.role: Boxer who won the fight against LaMotta.

timeline.event: Jake LaMotta loses to Billy Fox by TKO in the fourth round.
Jake LaMotta publishes 'Raging Bull', confessing to throwing the fight.

sources.author: Jeffrey T. Sammons
sources.work: Beyond the Ring: The Role of Boxing in American Society
sources.title: Jeffrey T. Sammons
